title: Probing the magnetic field of a coronal mass ejection with PSR J1022+1001
comment: Submitted to A&A on the 17 of march 2026
summary: We investigate whether low-frequency pulsar observations can provide LoS magnetic field estimates and whether these are consistent with synthetic LoS signatures extracted from a three-dimensional CME reconstruction constrained by Solar Orbiter data. We analyze a CME occultation of the LoS to PSR J1022+1001 on 20 August 2021, observed simultaneously with LOFAR and NenuFAR. From LOFAR, we derive time-resolved dispersion measure (DM) and rotation measure (RM) and isolate the CME contributions using background estimates for interstellar, solar wind and ionospheric components. We then infer the density-weighted LoS-averaged magnetic field component <B||>_PSR from the ratio delta-RM/delta-DM. In parallel, we reconstruct the CME using a semi-empirical 3DCORE model fitted to Solar Orbiter in-situ magnetic field observations at 0.65 au. We sample the modeled magnetic field along the pulsar LoS using fixed spatial sampling points and compute synthetic LoS-averaged signatures <B||>_3D for different flux rope configurations. The derived <B||>_PSR increases from approximately -9 nT to a peak near 63 nT during the observed interval. Comparison with synthetic signatures shows that the polarity and temporal evolution of the LoS signal are strongly dependent on the flux rope configuration and only a South-West-North (SWN) configuration (confirmed by Solar Orbiter in-situ data) reproduces the observed sign and overall evolution, whereas alternative configurations are incompatible. The modeled amplitudes, however, are systematically larger than the pulsar-derived values by roughly a factor of five. We show that simultaneous low-frequency pulsar DM and RM measurements can provide LoS magnetic field estimates for a CME and can be used to test CME magnetic structure against data-constrained three-dimensional reconstructions.

AI.tldr: Low-frequency pulsar observations (LOFAR/NenuFAR) provide line-of-sight magnetic field estimates of a CME, which are compared to 3D CME reconstructions from Solar Orbiter data, showing consistency in polarity/evolution but amplitude discrepancies.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether low-frequency pulsar observations can provide reliable line-of-sight (LoS) magnetic field estimates for coronal mass ejections (CMEs) and whether these estimates are consistent with synthetic signatures from 3D CME reconstructions constrained by in-situ spacecraft data.
AI.method: Analyzed CME occultation of PSR J1022+1001 on 20 August 2021 using simultaneous LOFAR and NenuFAR observations. Derived time-resolved dispersion measure (DM) and rotation measure (RM), isolated CME contributions from background components, and inferred density-weighted LoS-averaged magnetic field <B||>_PSR from delta-RM/delta-DM ratio. Reconstructed CME using semi-empirical 3DCORE model fitted to Solar Orbiter in-situ magnetic field data at 0.65 au. Sampled modeled magnetic field along pulsar LoS to compute synthetic LoS-averaged signatures <B||>_3D for different flux rope configurations.
AI.result: Derived <B||>_PSR increased from ~-9 nT to peak near 63 nT during observation. Comparison with synthetic signatures shows polarity and temporal evolution strongly depend on flux rope configuration. Only South-West-North (SWN) configuration (confirmed by Solar Orbiter data) reproduces observed sign and overall evolution, while alternative configurations are incompatible. However, modeled amplitudes are systematically larger than pulsar-derived values by roughly factor of five.
AI.conclusion: Simultaneous low-frequency pulsar DM and RM measurements can provide LoS magnetic field estimates for CMEs and can be used to test CME magnetic structure against data-constrained 3D reconstructions, though amplitude discrepancies suggest need for further refinement in modeling or observational techniques.

title: TESS light curves of two new magnetic cataclysmic variables: an asynchronous polar at the period minimum, and an eclipsing system with a large spin-to-orbit ratio
comment: Submitted to AAS journals and reviewed favorably; comments and suggestions welcome. Final revised version will be submitted before the end of March
summary: A recent development in the study of magnetic cataclysmic variable stars (mCVs) has been the identification of asynchronously spinning mCVs with orbital periods <2 h that have significantly higher white dwarf spin-to-orbital period ratios than their longer-period counterparts. We report the discovery of two additional mCVs in this class. The first, Gaia21akb, is a candidate asynchronous polar at the period minimum. While TESS photometry cannot, in isolation, lead to a conclusive identification of the orbital period, the probable orbital period of 1.29 h would be the second-shortest of any known polar and would result in a spin-to-orbit ratio of 0.9879. The second system in our study, ZTF18aazmehw, is an eclipsing mCV with a 1.50 h orbital period and a spin-to-orbit ratio of 0.867. Contrary to expectations for an asynchronous polar, ZTF18aazmehw does not show discernible evidence of pole switching and might possess a disk-like structure. The increasing number of short-period asynchronous mCVs with large spin-to-orbit ratios lends credence to theoretical predictions that asynchronously rotating mCVs with sufficiently strong white dwarf magnetic fields can achieve synchronization when their orbital separations have shrunk sufficiently.

AI.tldr: Discovery of two short-period asynchronous magnetic cataclysmic variables with high spin-to-orbit ratios, including a candidate polar near period minimum and an eclipsing system with unusual characteristics.
AI.motivation: Recent identification of short-period (<2 h) asynchronous mCVs with unusually high white dwarf spin-to-orbital period ratios compared to longer-period systems, suggesting new insights into synchronization mechanisms in magnetic cataclysmic variables.
AI.method: Analysis of TESS photometry for Gaia21akb and study of ZTF18aazmehw, an eclipsing mCV, to determine orbital periods and spin-to-orbit ratios through observational data analysis.
AI.result: Discovery of two new short-period asynchronous mCVs: Gaia21akb (probable orbital period 1.29 h, spin-to-orbit ratio 0.9879) and ZTF18aazmehw (orbital period 1.50 h, spin-to-orbit ratio 0.867). ZTF18aazmehw shows no evidence of pole switching and may have disk-like structure.
AI.conclusion: Increasing population of short-period asynchronous mCVs with large spin-to-orbit ratios supports theoretical predictions that sufficiently strong white dwarf magnetic fields can achieve synchronization as orbital separations shrink sufficiently.

title: Interferometric Images of the Starspot Evolution of $ζ$ Andromedae
comment: 21 pages, 15 figures, 6 tables; accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: The evolution of starspots of the giant primaries of RS CVn systems is typically detected indirectly with photometric and spectroscopic monitoring. These observations suggest slowly-evolving stellar surfaces and can constrain differential rotation as starspots move with respect to one another. However, starspot latitudes are difficult to constrain without resolved images of the stellar surfaces from which the unambiguous locations of starspots are determined. We imaged the active RS CVn primary $ζ$ And with the 330-m-baseline Center for High Angular Resolution Astronomy Array for three epochs over approximately six rotations of the star. The resultant images show a more complicated picture of stellar activity than expected from the contemporaneous photometry and earlier Doppler images. The spot structures change on the timescale of rotation, making differential rotation difficult to study. Our observations show changes in the polar spot, growing over time. We do not detect the secondary star in the interferometric data, though the observations are sensitive to the predicted 0.75 $M_\odot$ main-sequence star, and we suggest the companion may be a white dwarf.

AI.tldr: Interferometric imaging of RS CVn star ζ And reveals rapidly evolving starspots and polar spot growth, challenging previous photometric/spectroscopic models and suggesting a white dwarf companion.
AI.motivation: To directly image starspots on RS CVn giant ζ And using interferometry, overcoming limitations of indirect photometric/spectroscopic methods that cannot constrain spot latitudes and provide ambiguous surface information.
AI.method: Used the 330-m-baseline CHARA Array to image ζ And over three epochs spanning approximately six stellar rotations, providing resolved images of stellar surfaces to determine unambiguous starspot locations.
AI.result: Images revealed more complex stellar activity than expected from contemporaneous photometry and earlier Doppler images: spot structures change on rotational timescales (making differential rotation difficult to study), polar spot grows over time, and no detection of predicted 0.75 M⊙ main-sequence companion, suggesting it may be a white dwarf.
AI.conclusion: Direct interferometric imaging provides fundamentally different picture of RS CVn starspot evolution than indirect methods, showing rapid spot evolution and polar spot growth, while companion non-detection suggests white dwarf nature rather than main-sequence star.

title: Mining the Kepler Field: Atmospheric Parameters, Bolometric Corrections, and Luminosities
comment: Accepted for publication in RNAAS. 4 pages, 1 figure. Machine-readable table available at https://zenodo.org/records/18620911
summary: The ~ 200,000 stars observed by the Kepler mission have provided unprecedented constraints across astrophysics. With the advent of modern spectroscopic and photometric surveys, new limits in stellar characterizations are within reach. In this work, we report a compilation of atmospheric parameters (Teff, logg, and [M/H]) for the Kepler stars by crossmatching with several spectroscopic and spectro-photometric surveys. We use these to calculate bolometric corrections, which combined with color-magnitude diagram (CMD) information from Gaia yield self-consistent luminosities on a survey-by-survey basis. These properties will aid in future explorations of Kepler data towards new astrophysical insights. We make our catalog publicly available online in Zenodo (doi:10.5281/zenodo.18620911).

AI.tldr: Compilation of atmospheric parameters for Kepler stars from spectroscopic surveys, used to calculate bolometric corrections and derive self-consistent luminosities with Gaia data.
AI.motivation: To leverage modern spectroscopic and photometric surveys to improve stellar characterizations of Kepler mission stars, enabling better astrophysical insights from Kepler data.
AI.method: Cross-matched Kepler stars with multiple spectroscopic and spectro-photometric surveys to compile atmospheric parameters (Teff, logg, [M/H]), calculated bolometric corrections, and combined with Gaia CMD information to derive self-consistent luminosities on a survey-by-survey basis.
AI.result: Created a publicly available catalog of atmospheric parameters and derived luminosities for Kepler stars, hosted on Zenodo with DOI 10.5281/zenodo.18620911.
AI.conclusion: The compiled stellar properties will facilitate future explorations of Kepler data for new astrophysical discoveries, providing improved constraints on stellar parameters through multi-survey cross-matching.

title: Lithium in Wide Binaries: Effective Temperature Governs Depletion while Rotation Plays a Minor Role
comment: 19 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: Using a sample of 116 wide binary systems as coeval and chemically homogeneous stellar pairs, we investigate the factors governing lithium depletion in main-sequence stars. We recover the well-established morphology of the lithium--effective temperature ($T_{\mathrm{eff}}$) relation, including the Li dip (6200--6600\,K), the Li plateau (6000--6200\,K), and a linear trend for cooler stars ($T_{\mathrm{eff}}$ $<$ 6000\,K), where lithium abundance increases by $\sim$0.15\,dex per 100\,K. We demonstrate that the apparent correlation between projected rotational velocity ($v\sin i$) and lithium abundance is secondary to the underlying $T_{\mathrm{eff}}$ dependence; $v\sin i$ is not an independent driver of lithium depletion in our sample. Notably, we identify an anomalous system within the Li dip where the primary star exhibits a $\sim$1.4\,dex lithium excess compared to its secondary companion at nearly identical $T_{\mathrm{eff}}$. We discuss two plausible origins for this anomaly: external enrichment via planetesimal accretion or planetary engulfment, and binary interactions with an unresolved tertiary companion. Our results confirm $T_{\mathrm{eff}}$ as the dominant parameter controlling lithium depletion, while highlighting that additional, non-standard processes can occasionally produce significant lithium enrichment.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 116 wide binary systems shows effective temperature is the primary factor controlling lithium depletion in main-sequence stars, with one anomalous system showing significant lithium enrichment possibly from planetesimal accretion or binary interactions.
AI.motivation: To investigate the factors governing lithium depletion in main-sequence stars using wide binary systems as coeval and chemically homogeneous stellar pairs, distinguishing between temperature effects and other potential drivers like rotation.
AI.method: Used a sample of 116 wide binary systems as coeval and chemically homogeneous stellar pairs to study lithium depletion patterns. Analyzed lithium abundance as a function of effective temperature and projected rotational velocity, examining correlations and identifying anomalous systems.
AI.result: Recovered the established lithium-temperature relation including the Li dip (6200-6600 K), Li plateau (6000-6200 K), and linear trend for cooler stars. Found that the apparent correlation between rotational velocity and lithium abundance is secondary to temperature dependence. Identified one anomalous system within the Li dip where the primary star shows ~1.4 dex lithium excess compared to its secondary companion at nearly identical temperature.
AI.conclusion: Effective temperature is confirmed as the dominant parameter controlling lithium depletion in main-sequence stars. However, additional non-standard processes like external enrichment via planetesimal accretion/planetary engulfment or binary interactions with unresolved tertiary companions can occasionally produce significant lithium enrichment, as evidenced by the anomalous system.

title: A stray light analysis for SO/PHI-HRT and an updated comparison of the inferred magnetic field with SDO/HMI
comment: Submitted to A&A
summary: Context. The High Resolution Telescope of the Polarimetric and Helioseismic Imager on Solar Orbiter (SO/PHI-HRT) operates in an extreme observational environment, observing the Sun as close as $0.28$ au. The high thermal load and large illuminating field puts high demands on the instrument in terms of both imaging performance and false light control. Aims. To characterise the amount of stray light (false light) within SO/PHI-HRT, apply a correction, and re-compare the data products with the Helioseismic and Magnetic Imager on the Solar Dynamics Observatory (SDO/HMI). Methods. We analyse solar limb profiles and a Mercury transit to quantify the amount of stray light and add a correction term when partially reconstructing the SO/PHI-HRT images. For the comparison with SDO/HMI we use data from the 2023 March Solar Orbiter inferior conjunction and compare the magnetic fields on a pixel-by-pixel basis. Results. Increased continuum intensity contrast in the quiet Sun, and darker intensity levels are found in strong magnetic features. Consequently, much stronger fields are inferred in these features. Comparing the stray light corrected data with that from the standard SDO/HMI data products results in a much closer agreement across all vector magnetic field components, particularly when the cadence and noise levels are identical. In most solar features, SO/PHI-HRT infers stronger fields than the SDO/HMI line-of-sight magnetograms. Compared to the vector magnetic field from SDO/HMI the two are very well aligned, with only slight differences in the strongest field regions (where $|\mathbf{B}|>1600$ G or $|\mathbf{B_{\text{LOS}}}|>1300$ G).

AI.tldr: SO/PHI-HRT stray light correction improves magnetic field measurements, achieving better agreement with SDO/HMI data.
AI.motivation: SO/PHI-HRT operates in extreme Solar Orbiter environment (0.28 au) with high thermal load and large illuminating field, requiring characterization and correction of stray light to ensure accurate imaging and magnetic field measurements.
AI.method: Analyzed solar limb profiles and Mercury transit to quantify stray light, applied correction term during partial image reconstruction. Compared with SDO/HMI using 2023 March inferior conjunction data, performing pixel-by-pixel magnetic field comparison.
AI.result: Stray light correction increased continuum intensity contrast in quiet Sun and darkened strong magnetic features, revealing stronger inferred magnetic fields. Corrected data shows much closer agreement with SDO/HMI across all vector magnetic field components, especially with matched cadence and noise levels. SO/PHI-HRT generally infers stronger fields than SDO/HMI LOS magnetograms, with excellent alignment in vector fields except slight differences in strongest field regions (>1600 G or >1300 G LOS).
AI.conclusion: Stray light correction significantly improves SO/PHI-HRT data quality and magnetic field measurements, achieving excellent agreement with SDO/HMI reference data, validating the instrument's performance in extreme Solar Orbiter conditions.

title: The MOND Depth Index and Dynamical Maturity Clock: Toward a Universal Classification of Galaxies and Star Clusters
comment: 17 pages, 5 figures
summary: Mass discrepancies in galaxies are empirically known to appear only below a characteristic acceleration scale a0. Here we show that this behaviour is not limited to galaxies: it extends continuously across the full hierarchy of self-gravitating stellar systems, from gas-rich dwarfs and spirals to massive early-type galaxies, and further down to compact stellar clusters. We introduce the Milgromian dynamics (MOND) depth index DM, together with dynamical maturity index T = tcross/tH, dynamical collisionality index T1 = tcross/trelax, with tcross being the crossing time, tH the Hubble time and trelax the median two-body relaxation time, and the MOND acceleration index A = abar/a0. We uncover a well-defined two-dimensional dividing surface in dynamical space. The "dark matter phenomenon" is found only in systems that are both in the deep-MOND regime (abar < a0) and collisionless (trelax > tH), while high-acceleration, collisional systems (abar > a0, trelax << tH), including globular clusters and UCDs, show no evidence for a mass discrepancy. This clean dynamical separation defines a new, physically motivated classification scheme for stellar systems, unifying galaxies and clusters under one framework. The observed division emerges naturally within the MOND framework and provides a useful diagnostic for examining how different gravitational paradigms account for the origin of the mass discrepancy.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the dark matter phenomenon appears only in systems with low acceleration (abar < a0) and long relaxation times (trelax > tH), while high-acceleration, collisional systems show no mass discrepancy, revealing a clean dynamical separation across all stellar systems.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that the mass discrepancy phenomenon (dark matter) extends continuously across the full hierarchy of self-gravitating stellar systems and is not limited to galaxies, and to establish a physically motivated classification scheme based on dynamical properties.
AI.method: Introduces three dimensionless indices: MOND depth index DM, dynamical maturity index T = tcross/tH, dynamical collisionality index T1 = tcross/trelax, and MOND acceleration index A = abar/a0. Analyzes the distribution of stellar systems in this dynamical parameter space to identify dividing surfaces.
AI.result: Uncovers a well-defined two-dimensional dividing surface: dark matter phenomenon appears only in systems that are both in the deep-MOND regime (abar < a0) and collisionless (trelax > tH). High-acceleration, collisional systems (abar > a0, trelax << tH) show no evidence for mass discrepancy.
AI.conclusion: The clean dynamical separation defines a new, physically motivated classification scheme for stellar systems that unifies galaxies and clusters under one framework. This division emerges naturally within MOND and provides a diagnostic for testing gravitational paradigms.

title: S2D2: Small-scale Significant substructure DBSCAN Detection II. Tracing episodes and gradients of star formation activity
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A
summary: We provide the community with a homogeneous catalogue of small, significant substructures (henceforth NESTs) extracted from the spatial distribution of Young Stellar Objects (YSOs) in a large, consistent sample of star-forming regions. The catalog allows us to explore the relevance of small scale spatial substructure and discuss the interpretation of NESTs as tracers of star formation activity and remnants of the star formation process. We apply our procedure to consistent catalogues of YSOs to obtain NESTs in a sample of star-forming regions. We apply a photometric classification scheme to obtain the evolutionary stage of YSOs and statistically explore the distribution of class 0/I objects as a proxy of recent star formation activity. The region sample is diverse (in distance, size, structure, and global evolutionary stage), and we consequently find different structural properties and star formation histories. Most NESTs in regions with high recent star formation activity show even higher levels of activity. Moreover, the proportion of NESTs with higher activity than the region average increases with the global level of activity of the region. In approximately half of the regions we also find significant spans in the evolutionary stages of the NESTs, consistent with gradients and episodes of star formation. The combination of NESTs with a statistical exploration of the star formation history within each region provides robust and powerful insights into the star formation process. Our results support the role of NESTs as pristine remnants of star formation in highly active regions,stressing the role of fragmentation. The combination of small structures with large scale spatio-evolutionary patterns suggests hyerarchical, prolonged, dynamic, and complex star formation scenarios.

AI.tldr: Researchers create a homogeneous catalog of small substructures (NESTs) from YSO distributions to study star formation patterns, finding hierarchical and prolonged star formation scenarios.
AI.motivation: To systematically study small-scale spatial substructures in star-forming regions as tracers of star formation activity and remnants of the star formation process.
AI.method: Extract NESTs from consistent YSO catalogs using photometric classification to determine evolutionary stages, statistically analyze class 0/I objects as proxies for recent star formation, and examine diverse regions with varying distances, sizes, structures, and evolutionary stages.
AI.result: Most NESTs in highly active regions show even higher activity levels; proportion of NESTs with above-average activity increases with regional activity; approximately half of regions show significant evolutionary stage spans indicating gradients and episodic star formation.
AI.conclusion: NESTs serve as pristine remnants of star formation in active regions, supporting fragmentation's role; combination of small structures with large-scale patterns suggests hierarchical, prolonged, dynamic, and complex star formation scenarios.

title: The Spectroscopic and Photometric Study of a Star Cluster Sample in Andromeda Halo
comment: 18 pages, 11 figures, 6 tables. Accepted for publication in AJ. Spectroscopic and photometric data are available
summary: Halo star clusters serve as vital tracers for the formation and evolution of the Andromeda galaxy. In this work, we present physical parameters for 29 M31 halo star clusters, derived from a combination of spectroscopic and photometric data. Low-resolution spectra were acquired using the BFOSC spectrograph on the NAOC Xinglong 2.16-m telescope. For the photometric analysis, we utilized uSC and vSAGE bands from the SAGE survey, complemented by archival data from GALEX(NUV, FUV), PAN-STARRS(grizy) and the 2MASS(JHK). Ages and metallicities were determined via ULySS (Vazdekis et al. and pegase-hr) SSP model and the Bruzual & Charlot (2003) (BC03) stellar population synthesis models. The derived parameters show good agreement with literature values. Notably, for three of these clusters, this study represents the first combined photometric and spectroscopic analysis.

AI.tldr: First combined photometric and spectroscopic analysis of 29 M31 halo star clusters using multi-wavelength data and stellar population models to determine physical parameters.
AI.motivation: Halo star clusters are crucial tracers for understanding the formation and evolution of the Andromeda galaxy (M31), but comprehensive physical parameter determinations combining both spectroscopic and photometric data are needed.
AI.method: Combined spectroscopic (BFOSC spectrograph on NAOC Xinglong 2.16-m telescope) and photometric analysis using uSC/vSAGE bands from SAGE survey, plus archival data from GALEX (NUV, FUV), PAN-STARRS (grizy), and 2MASS (JHK). Parameters determined using ULySS SSP models (Vazdekis et al. and pegase-hr) and BC03 stellar population synthesis models.
AI.result: Derived physical parameters for 29 M31 halo star clusters showing good agreement with literature values. Three clusters received their first combined photometric and spectroscopic analysis.
AI.conclusion: The study provides reliable physical parameters for M31 halo clusters using multi-wavelength data and stellar population modeling, establishing a foundation for understanding M31's formation history through its halo cluster population.

title: Evolution of Nuclear Star Cluster in Dwarf Galaxy through Mergers and In-Situ Star Formation
comment: 30 pages, 17 pages, 2 tables, accepted in ApJ
summary: Nuclear Star Clusters (NSCs) are dense stellar systems located at the centers of galaxies. Employing Enzo-Abyss, which integrates hydrodynamics with a direct N-body solver, we introduce a simulation capable of resolving the evolution of NSCs within a live galaxy. This includes live dark matter, gaseous dynamics, star formation and feedback, collisional dynamics for star clusters. The evolution of NSCs is typically shaped by two main processes: mergers of star clusters and in-situ star formation. Our simulation enables investigation of the contributions of these mechanisms to the growth of NSCs. This work focuses on the impact of stellar physics and gas content on the growth of NSCs within a dwarf galaxy. To this end, we carry out four simulations, a fiducial simulation, one without supernova feedback, one with low star formation efficiency, and one with higher galactic gas content. This study shows a likelihood that both mergers and in-situ star formation contribute to NSC evolution comparably. In addition, mergers result in disruption of dense gas clumps within star clusters, indicating that in-situ star formation is suppressed when mergers occur. However, the limitations -- such as the lack of individual star physics and limited spatial/particle mass resolution -- hinder drawing a definite conclusion. Nevertheless, with further development, our simulations will serve as a cornerstone that untangles the complex interplay between mergers and in-situ star formation in shaping the structure and mass of NSCs, thereby providing insights into their formation and evolution.

AI.tldr: Simulation study using Enzo-Abyss to model Nuclear Star Cluster evolution in dwarf galaxies, examining contributions from cluster mergers vs. in-situ star formation under varying stellar physics and gas conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand the relative contributions of star cluster mergers and in-situ star formation to Nuclear Star Cluster growth in dwarf galaxies, and how stellar physics and gas content affect these processes.
AI.method: Used Enzo-Abyss hydrodynamics + direct N-body solver to simulate NSC evolution within live galaxy (dark matter, gas dynamics, star formation, feedback). Conducted four simulations: fiducial, no supernova feedback, low star formation efficiency, and higher galactic gas content.
AI.result: Both mergers and in-situ star formation likely contribute comparably to NSC evolution. Mergers disrupt dense gas clumps within star clusters, suppressing in-situ star formation when mergers occur.
AI.conclusion: While limitations prevent definitive conclusions, the simulation framework serves as a cornerstone for understanding the complex interplay between mergers and in-situ star formation in shaping NSC structure and mass, providing insights into their formation and evolution.

title: The Galactic distribution of planetary nebulae with different types of dust
comment: 21 pages, 10 figures, 4 tables, accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: We identify different dust features in our compilation of infrared spectra for 267 planetary nebulae (PNe) from the Spitzer, ISO, and IRAS telescopes. We classify 206 objects according to their dust type: mixed dust (MD), oxygen-rich dust (ORD), carbon-rich dust (CRD), PNe with only polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) in their spectra (oPAH), and featureless (F) PNe. We study statistically the distributions of surface brightness and diameter of PNe with different types of dust as well as their distributions in the Galaxy.We find that both MD and ORD PNe are closer to the Galactic centre than CRD and oPAH PNe, and that the Galactic distributions of each pair of groups are statistically compatible, suggesting that they have similar progenitors. Since oPAH PNe have, on average, larger diameters and lower surface brightness than CRD PNe, we suggest that oPAH PNe are evolved CRD PNe. On the other hand, F PNe have the lowest surface brightness and the largest diameters, suggesting they could contain evolved PNe from any initial type of dust. Among the PNe with silicates, we find that only a few ORD PNe have just amorphous silicates in their spectra, and their distributions of Galactocentric distances and Galactic heights suggest that they had low-mass progenitors. MD PNe with both amorphous and crystalline silicates have the largest surface brightness and the smallest diameters and might be the earliest stages of PNe with the most massive and metal-rich progenitors.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 267 planetary nebulae reveals five dust types with distinct Galactic distributions and evolutionary implications, suggesting connections between dust composition, progenitor mass/metallicity, and nebular evolution.
AI.motivation: To understand the relationship between dust composition in planetary nebulae and their evolutionary stages, progenitor characteristics, and Galactic distribution by systematically classifying infrared dust features across a large sample.
AI.method: Compiled infrared spectra from Spitzer, ISO, and IRAS telescopes for 267 planetary nebulae; classified objects into five dust types based on spectral features; performed statistical analysis of surface brightness, diameter, and Galactic distributions for each group.
AI.result: Identified 206 classified objects: mixed dust (MD), oxygen-rich dust (ORD), carbon-rich dust (CRD), PAH-only (oPAH), and featureless (F) PNe. Found MD and ORD PNe are closer to Galactic center than CRD and oPAH PNe. oPAH PNe have larger diameters and lower surface brightness than CRD PNe, suggesting evolutionary sequence. F PNe are largest and faintest, likely evolved from any dust type. ORD with only amorphous silicates have low-mass progenitor signatures. MD PNe with crystalline silicates are brightest and smallest, indicating massive, metal-rich progenitors.
AI.conclusion: Dust composition in planetary nebulae correlates with progenitor mass, metallicity, and evolutionary stage. MD PNe represent early stages with massive, metal-rich progenitors; ORD PNe with amorphous silicates have low-mass progenitors; oPAH PNe are evolved CRD PNe; and F PNe are the most evolved remnants. Galactic distributions reveal systematic relationships between dust type and progenitor population characteristics.

title: Contrasting evolutionary pathways of fast- and slow-rotating galaxies in the green valley
comment: 18 pages, 10 figures; accepted for publication in A&A; abstract shortened
summary: We investigate the evolutionary pathways of green valley (GV) galaxies drawn from the SDSS-IV/MaNGA survey. The GV sample is divided into fast- and slow-rotating galaxies based on stellar spin, and their stellar and gas-phase metallicities are compared. Fast-rotating galaxies exhibit systematically higher metallicities than slow-rotating galaxies in both gas and stars. However, the gas-phase difference is significant only at low stellar masses, while the stellar metallicity offset persists across the full mass range. Using a simple yet physically motivated chemical evolution model, optimised to jointly fit gas-phase metallicities and integrated stellar spectra, we reconstruct the star formation and chemical enrichment histories of individual galaxies and constrain gas inflow and outflow parameters. At low stellar masses, fast- and slow-rotating galaxies show similar gas-infall and star formation timescales, but the the slower population experienced stronger outflows which reduce their chemical content in both gas and stars. At high masses, the combination of reduced pristine gas inflow and more efficient gas removal in slow-rotating galaxies produce gas-phase metallicities comparable to fast-rotating galaxies but systematically lower stellar metallicities. These differences suggest distinct evolutionary pathways for GV galaxies. Slow-rotating galaxies likely experienced more mergers, usually associated with strong gas removal processes, leading to their systematically lower metallicities. At low masses, stronger supernova-driven outflows reduce their chemical content while leaving star-formation timescales similar to fast-rotating galaxies. At high masses, merger-triggered AGN feedback may rapidly deplete and suppress gas infall, producing the shorter star-formation timescales seen in slow-rotating galaxies. Alternative environmental and assembly-driven scenarios are also discussed.

AI.tldr: Fast-rotating green valley galaxies have higher metallicities than slow-rotating ones, with differences explained by distinct evolutionary pathways involving mergers, outflows, and AGN feedback.
AI.motivation: To understand the evolutionary pathways of green valley galaxies by comparing fast- and slow-rotating populations and investigating how their different rotation properties relate to chemical enrichment histories.
AI.method: Divided SDSS-IV/MaNGA green valley galaxies into fast- and slow-rotating based on stellar spin, compared stellar and gas-phase metallicities, and used a chemical evolution model optimized to jointly fit gas-phase metallicities and integrated stellar spectra to reconstruct star formation and chemical enrichment histories.
AI.result: Fast-rotating galaxies show systematically higher metallicities than slow-rotating galaxies in both gas and stars. At low masses, slow-rotating galaxies experienced stronger outflows reducing chemical content. At high masses, slow-rotating galaxies had reduced pristine gas inflow and more efficient gas removal, producing comparable gas-phase but lower stellar metallicities.
AI.conclusion: Distinct evolutionary pathways exist for green valley galaxies: slow-rotating galaxies likely experienced more mergers with strong gas removal processes, leading to systematically lower metallicities through different mechanisms at low (supernova-driven outflows) and high (merger-triggered AGN feedback) masses.

title: Learning to See Sharper: A Physics-Informed Artificial Intelligence Framework for Super-Resolving Galaxy Spectra
comment: 16 Pages, 9 Figures, Submitted to ApJ
summary: The information recoverable from galaxy spectra depends fundamentally on spectral resolution, yet assembling large samples at high resolution remains observationally expensive. We present a deep-learning framework for spectral super-resolution that enhances low-resolution galaxy spectra by a factor of $\sim$10 in resolving power ($R\sim100$ to $R\sim1000$). The model is trained on 1,187 paired JWST/NIRSpec observations from the JADES program, where low-resolution prism spectra are matched with medium-resolution grating spectra (G140M, G235M, G395M) combined into a unified reference covering 1-5 $μ$m. Our three-stage architecture performs an initial super-resolution, infers the redshift from the coarse reconstruction, and then applies a physics-informed residual refinement that uses attention across emission-line tokens to learn inter-line relationships and predict parametric line profiles, alongside a convolutional branch for continuum corrections. Evaluated on a 20% held-out sample, the model achieves noise-limited residuals over most of the spectral range and systematically improves the signal-to-noise ratio of key diagnostic lines including [OII], H$β$, [OIII], and H$α$, often by factors of several. The super-resolved spectra successfully deblend features that are entirely unresolved at prism resolution, such as the [OIII] $λ\lambda4959,5007$ doublet and H$β$. As a proof of concept using JWST data, this approach is readily extensible to the low-resolution grism spectroscopy that will be delivered by Euclid and the Roman Space Telescope, potentially enabling population-level diagnostics across millions of galaxy spectra that would otherwise be inaccessible at grism resolution.

AI.tldr: Deep learning framework enhances JWST galaxy spectra resolution from R~100 to R~1000 using paired prism/grating observations, improving line detection and deblending for future large surveys.
AI.motivation: High-resolution galaxy spectra provide crucial astrophysical information but are observationally expensive to obtain for large samples. Low-resolution spectroscopy from upcoming missions like Euclid and Roman Space Telescope will produce millions of spectra, but their diagnostic power is limited by resolution.
AI.method: Three-stage deep learning architecture: 1) initial super-resolution, 2) redshift inference from coarse reconstruction, 3) physics-informed residual refinement using attention mechanisms across emission-line tokens to learn inter-line relationships and predict parametric line profiles, plus convolutional branch for continuum corrections. Trained on 1,187 paired JWST/NIRSpec observations from JADES program.
AI.result: Model achieves noise-limited residuals over most spectral range, improves SNR of key diagnostic lines ([OII], Hβ, [OIII], Hα) by factors of several, successfully deblends features unresolved at prism resolution (e.g., [OIII] doublet and Hβ).
AI.conclusion: The spectral super-resolution framework successfully enhances low-resolution spectra by factor ~10, enabling access to population-level diagnostics across millions of galaxy spectra from upcoming missions that would otherwise be inaccessible at grism resolution.

title: SDSS-IV MaNGA: Distinct Structural Growth and Star Formation in Low and High Surface Brightness Disks
comment: 31 pages, 19 figures, accepted for publication in the ApJ Supplement
summary: We analyze a clean sample of 1,118 late-type, face-on galaxies without AGN contamination from the MaNGA survey. Their photometric structures are quantified via two-component (bulge+disk) decompositions on deep $g$-band images from the DESI Legacy Survey. Using a disk central surface brightness of $μ_{\rm 0,d,cor}$(g) = 22 $\pm$ 0.3 mag arcsec$^{-2}$ (corrected for inclination and cosmic dimming) as the classification threshold, we identify 159 low surface brightness (LSB) galaxies, 388 LSB candidates, and 571 high surface brightness (HSB) galaxies. LSB galaxies are predominantly low-mass ($M_\ast < 3 \times 10^{10}$ M$_\odot$), exhibiting 29\% larger effective radii, 15\% lower star formation rates (SFRs), and 12\% reduced gas-phase metallicities than HSB counterparts at comparable masses. These differences cause systematic offsets from standard scaling relations. Despite comparable gas content, LSB galaxies host older stellar populations, longer gas depletion times, and less efficient star formation. Spatially resolved analyses further reveal that LSB galaxies display centrally suppressed $Σ_{\rm SFR}$, flatter SFR gradients, and rising specific SFR profiles toward their outskirts. Together with steeper negative metallicity gradients, these trends suggest ongoing gas accretion fueling outer-disk star formation. Consistently, the outer regions of LSB galaxies exhibit stronger H$δ_A$ absorption and lower D$_n$4000 indices, indicating fading A-star populations. Moreover, LSB galaxies show lower $Σ_{\ast}$ across all $R/R_e$ and more centrally depleted stellar mass profiles on an absolute radial scale, compared with HSB and large-size star-forming galaxies. Collectively, LSB galaxies represent a distinct population with slow evolution, inefficient star formation, and continued susceptibility to late-time gas accretion and peripheral star formation.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 1,118 face-on late-type galaxies from MaNGA reveals LSB galaxies as a distinct population with larger sizes, lower SFRs, suppressed central star formation, flatter SFR gradients, and evidence of ongoing gas accretion fueling outer-disk star formation.
AI.motivation: To systematically characterize the properties of low surface brightness (LSB) galaxies compared to high surface brightness (HSB) galaxies, understand their distinct evolutionary pathways, and investigate how they deviate from standard galaxy scaling relations.
AI.method: Used a clean sample of 1,118 late-type, face-on galaxies without AGN contamination from MaNGA survey. Performed two-component (bulge+disk) photometric decompositions on deep g-band images from DESI Legacy Survey. Classified galaxies using disk central surface brightness threshold of μ₀,d,cor(g) = 22 ± 0.3 mag arcsec⁻². Conducted spatially resolved analyses of star formation rates, metallicity gradients, stellar populations, and mass profiles.
AI.result: Identified 159 LSB galaxies, 388 LSB candidates, and 571 HSB galaxies. LSB galaxies are predominantly low-mass (M* < 3×10¹⁰ M⊙), with 29% larger effective radii, 15% lower SFRs, and 12% reduced gas-phase metallicities than HSB counterparts. Show centrally suppressed ΣSFR, flatter SFR gradients, rising specific SFR profiles toward outskirts, steeper negative metallicity gradients, older stellar populations, longer gas depletion times, and more centrally depleted stellar mass profiles.
AI.conclusion: LSB galaxies represent a distinct population with slow evolution, inefficient star formation, and continued susceptibility to late-time gas accretion and peripheral star formation. Their systematic offsets from standard scaling relations and unique structural properties suggest different evolutionary pathways compared to HSB galaxies.

title: UGC 2369S: a Kpc Scale Triple Merger Candidate Identified in a Nearby Luminous Infrared Galaxy
comment: 26 pages, 17+3 figures, 2 tables. Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: We present high spatial resolution ($\lesssim$1.0''), multi-wavelength observations of UGC 2369S, a nearby luminous infrared galaxy showing three distinct cores separated on kpc scales in near-infrared (NIR) imaging with significant X-ray emission. Utilizing optical/NIR adaptive optics (AO), radio, \chandra X-ray, as well as archival HST imaging, we perform a comprehensive study of AGN activity, obscuration, and host properties. As one of the clearest cases of a triple-nucleus merger at $\simeq$3 kpc separations, UGC 2369S is the first to be studied with high-resolution observations at multiple wavelength. We find that the northern core, having possibly the most massive black hole in the system ($\rm M_{BH}\simeq10^{8}\,M_{\odot}$) is consistent with a heavily obscured AGN. However, its high dust extinction ($\rm A_v>5$), hydrogen column density ($N_\mathrm{H}\gtrsim 10^{25}\,\rm cm^{-2}$) and non-detection of optical coronal lines and coronal X-ray emission leave the identification inconclusive. The other two cores show no evidence for black-hole activity and instead exhibit signatures of tidal disruption. From stellar mass surface density and stellar velocity dispersion maps, we infer that the strongly varying gravitational potential in this three-body system may have cannibalized the stellar bulge of the southwestern core, leaving a metal enriched remnant. An ongoing survey focusing on similar triple systems could help us understand how they evolve and help benchmark numerical simulations, providing insight into gravitational wave predictions and the formation of the most massive black holes.

AI.tldr: Multi-wavelength study of UGC 2369S reveals a triple-nucleus merger with one heavily obscured AGN candidate and two cores showing tidal disruption signatures, providing insights into multi-body galactic interactions.
AI.motivation: To investigate AGN activity, obscuration, and host properties in UGC 2369S as a rare triple-nucleus merger system, which can provide insights into multi-body galactic interactions and black hole evolution.
AI.method: Utilized high spatial resolution (≲1.0'') multi-wavelength observations including optical/NIR adaptive optics, radio, Chandra X-ray, and archival HST imaging to perform comprehensive analysis of the three distinct cores.
AI.result: The northern core shows characteristics of a heavily obscured AGN (M_BH∼10⁸ M⊙, A_v>5, N_H≳10²⁵ cm⁻²) but lacks definitive coronal line/X-ray detection. The other two cores show no AGN activity but exhibit tidal disruption signatures, with the southwestern core possibly having its stellar bulge cannibalized by the three-body gravitational potential.
AI.conclusion: UGC 2369S represents a clear triple-nucleus merger case where complex gravitational interactions affect black hole activity and stellar structure evolution, highlighting the need for systematic studies of similar systems to benchmark simulations and understand massive black hole formation.

title: Emission-line Variable Active Galactic Nuclei at Cosmic Noon from HETDEX
comment: 22 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables, accepted by the Astrophysical Journal Supplement Series
summary: We present the first statistical census of emission-line variable active galactic nuclei (EVA) at cosmic noon by combining untargeted and deep HETDEX spectroscopy with multi-epoch spectra from SDSS, DESI, and LAMOST. Anchoring all candidates to a HETDEX spectroscopic epoch and requiring AGN classification in either the HETDEX or the external epoch(s), we identify a homogeneous sample of 100 EVA at z~1.5, including 98 newly identified. Emission-line variability is selected primarily through statistically significant line-flux changes, supplemented by extensive visual inspections using contemporaneous photometric light curves. The resulting incidence fraction is $f_{\rm EVA} \approx 0.9\%$. The rest-frame intervals between spectroscopic epochs span $\sim$1--10 yr, with brightening and dimming events exhibiting statistically indistinguishable characteristic timescales ($ΔT\sim2.2$ and $\sim2.6$ yr, respectively). A key result is the characterization of the Baldwin effect in the time domain: while many EVA follow the ensemble Baldwin effect (eBeff) between two epochs, a substantial fraction exhibit apparent anti-eBeff responses. Time-resolved spectroscopy of an individual source reveals that the intrinsic EW--luminosity relation is non-stationary, with the line-to-continuum responsivity systematically evolving from stronger to weaker across successive variability cycles; sparse two-epoch sampling of this evolving intrinsic Baldwin evolution (iBeff) naturally produces both eBeff-like and anti-eBeff behaviors. Finally, EVA show no strong preference for extreme Eddington ratios but exhibit a mild tendency toward lower $λ_{\rm Edd}$ values relative to matched control samples, driven primarily by sources observed in their dim states. Together, these results establish a coherent framework for interpreting emission-line variability in AGN at the peak epoch of cosmic black hole growth.

AI.tldr: First statistical census of emission-line variable AGN at cosmic noon (z~1.5) reveals ~0.9% incidence fraction, characterizes Baldwin effect in time domain, and shows evolving line-to-continuum responsivity across variability cycles.
AI.motivation: To establish the first statistical census of emission-line variable active galactic nuclei (EVA) during cosmic noon (z~1.5), a peak epoch of cosmic black hole growth, by combining deep multi-epoch spectroscopy from multiple surveys to study AGN variability patterns and their physical implications.
AI.method: Combined untargeted deep HETDEX spectroscopy with multi-epoch spectra from SDSS, DESI, and LAMOST. Anchored all candidates to a HETDEX spectroscopic epoch and required AGN classification in either HETDEX or external epochs. Selected emission-line variability through statistically significant line-flux changes supplemented by visual inspections using contemporaneous photometric light curves. Identified homogeneous sample of 100 EVA at z~1.5 (98 newly identified).
AI.result: Incidence fraction f_EVA ≈ 0.9%. Rest-frame intervals between spectroscopic epochs span ~1-10 years with brightening and dimming events showing statistically indistinguishable characteristic timescales (ΔT~2.2 and ~2.6 years). Characterized Baldwin effect in time domain: while many EVA follow ensemble Baldwin effect (eBeff), substantial fraction exhibit apparent anti-eBeff responses. Time-resolved spectroscopy reveals intrinsic EW-luminosity relation is non-stationary, with line-to-continuum responsivity systematically evolving from stronger to weaker across successive variability cycles. EVA show no strong preference for extreme Eddington ratios but exhibit mild tendency toward lower λ_Edd values relative to matched control samples, driven primarily by sources observed in dim states.
AI.conclusion: Establishes coherent framework for interpreting emission-line variability in AGN at peak epoch of cosmic black hole growth. Sparse two-epoch sampling of evolving intrinsic Baldwin evolution (iBeff) naturally produces both eBeff-like and anti-eBeff behaviors, explaining observed variability patterns. Results provide foundation for understanding AGN physics during cosmic noon through emission-line variability studies.

title: The edge of the Milky Way's star-forming disc: Evidence from a 'U-shaped' stellar age profile
summary: We leveraged reliable age and distance estimates from LAMOST-DR3 and APOGEE-DR17+AstroNN combined with \gaia\ data to perform a detailed analysis of the stellar age distribution in the Milky Way's (MW) outer disc using giant stars. Selecting stars near the midplane ($|z|<0.3$ kpc) on near-circular orbits ($λ_c > 0.9$), we analysed these independent datasets that employed different age-estimation methods. Our stringent kinematic selection criteria effectively exclude halo stars, ensuring that the observed age trends reflect genuine disc properties rather than contamination from older halo populations. Our results reveal a 'U-shaped' stellar age profile, where a negative gradient in the inner disc transitions to a positive gradient in the outer disc region. We identify the minimum in the stellar age profile at $R_{\rm min}=11.28 \pm 0.58$ kpc and $R_{\rm min}=12.15\pm 0.62$ kpc for the APOGEE-DR17 and LAMOST-DR3 samples, respectively. Using N-body+SPH simulations, we demonstrate that $R_{\rm min}$ corresponds to the break radius in the stellar density profile ($R_{\rm br}$), marking the edge of the Galaxy's star-forming disc. This break arises from a sharp decline in the star formation rate, with the outer positive age gradient produced by the radial migration of stars born inside $R_{\rm br}$. The cessation of star formation in the outer disc might be due to several mechanisms, including the dynamical influence of the bar's outer Lindblad resonance, the onset of the Galactic warp, or thermally regulated star formation. Overall, our results support the picture that the MW has a Type II (down-bending) stellar disc with a break at $R_{\rm br} \approx 11.28-12.15$ kpc, where the combination of star-formation cut-off and radial migration produces the observed U-shaped age profile.

AI.tldr: The Milky Way's outer disc exhibits a U-shaped stellar age profile with a minimum at 11-12 kpc, corresponding to the break radius where star formation ceases and radial migration creates the observed pattern.
AI.motivation: To understand the stellar age distribution in the Milky Way's outer disc and determine the relationship between age profiles, break radii, and star formation history using multiple spectroscopic surveys.
AI.method: Combined LAMOST-DR3 and APOGEE-DR17+AstroNN data with Gaia for reliable age/distance estimates. Selected midplane giant stars ($|z|<0.3$ kpc) on near-circular orbits ($λ_c > 0.9$) to exclude halo contamination. Used N-body+SPH simulations to interpret observational results.
AI.result: Found U-shaped age profile with negative gradient in inner disc transitioning to positive gradient in outer disc. Identified minimum at $R_{\rm min}=11.28\pm0.58$ kpc (APOGEE) and $12.15\pm0.62$ kpc (LAMOST). This minimum corresponds to break radius $R_{\rm br}$ marking star-forming disc edge.
AI.conclusion: The Milky Way has a Type II (down-bending) disc with break at $R_{\rm br} \approx 11-12$ kpc. The U-shaped age profile results from star formation cessation at $R_{\rm br}$ combined with radial migration of stars born inside the break radius.

title: Kinematic diagnostics for non-axisymmetry in the Milky Way's nuclear stellar disc
summary: There is now strong evidence that the Milky Way (MW) hosts a nuclear stellar disc (NSD). However, whether the NSD is purely axisymmetric or contains a nuclear bar remains unresolved. Since approximately $50\%$ of barred galaxies with MW-like mass in the local Universe host a nuclear bar, investigating whether the MW hosts one is of interest. We conduct a systematic analysis to identify robust kinematic diagnostics capable of determining whether the MW hosts a nuclear bar. Using N-body simulations, we explore the kinematic signatures indicative of a nuclear bar. Using the phase-space coordinates longitude $(\ell)$, latitude $(b)$, proper motions ($μ_\ell$ and $μ_{\rm b})$ and line-of-sight velocity $(v_{\rm los})$, we test various diagnostics assuming different nuclear bar orientations. We also evaluate how sample size, dust extinction and bar amplitude influence the efficacy of the diagnostics. We identify two independent kinematic diagnostics capable of revealing a nuclear bar in the MW: (1) the vertex deviation, $l_{\rm v}$, of the ($v_{\ell}-v_{\rm los}$) velocity ellipse; and (2) The asymmetry in the $μ_{\ell}$ vs $\ell$ distribution. While both are impacted by the sample size and extinction, the vertex deviation proves more robust, especially when combining stars from multiple observational fields. We also assess the correlation between the line-of-sight velocity and the $h_3$ Gauss-Hermite moment ("skewness") of the line-of-sight velocity but find no clear distinction between an NSD and a nuclear bar based on this metric. Our results suggest that data from the current KMOS survey may allow a marginal detection of a nuclear bar using the vertex deviation method. A companion paper provides further validation and detailed analysis of this approach. Nonetheless, future surveys will provide the high quality data necessary to fully exploit the diagnostics outlined in this study.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies robust kinematic diagnostics to determine if the Milky Way hosts a nuclear bar, finding vertex deviation and proper motion asymmetry as key indicators, with current data allowing marginal detection.
AI.motivation: While there's strong evidence for a nuclear stellar disc in the Milky Way, it's unclear whether it contains a nuclear bar. Since ~50% of barred galaxies with MW-like mass host nuclear bars, investigating this possibility is scientifically important to understand the Milky Way's structure.
AI.method: Used N-body simulations to explore kinematic signatures of nuclear bars. Analyzed phase-space coordinates (longitude, latitude, proper motions, line-of-sight velocity) with various diagnostics assuming different nuclear bar orientations. Evaluated effects of sample size, dust extinction, and bar amplitude on diagnostic efficacy.
AI.result: Identified two independent kinematic diagnostics: (1) vertex deviation (l_v) of the (v_ℓ-v_los) velocity ellipse, and (2) asymmetry in μ_ℓ vs ℓ distribution. Vertex deviation proved more robust, especially when combining stars from multiple fields. Found no clear distinction using h_3 Gauss-Hermite moment correlation with line-of-sight velocity. Current KMOS survey data may allow marginal detection via vertex deviation.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical kinematic diagnostics for detecting a nuclear bar in the Milky Way, with vertex deviation being the most robust method. While current data may allow marginal detection, future surveys will provide the high-quality data needed to fully exploit these diagnostics and resolve whether the Milky Way hosts a nuclear bar.

title: Spin-up and spin distribution of stellar black holes grown by gas accretion in proto-stellar clusters
comment: to appear in A&A
summary: Proto-stellar clusters, likely progenitors of globular clusters, are compact with typical mass $\sim 10^6\,{\rm M}_\odot$ and size $\sim 1\,{\rm pc}$, as revealed recently by JWST observations at $z\sim 10$. Sufficiently high compactness can provide a time window for early-formed stellar black holes (BHs) to accrete primordial gas. We develop a model to determine the final spin distribution of stellar BHs which grow in mass via gas accretion within compact gaseous proto-stellar clusters. The velocity shear within a BH's sphere of influence induces the formation of an accretion disk which is repeatedly disrupted by stochastic perturbations to the BH motion. We assume low initial BH spins $a_{*,{\rm ini}} = 0.01$, and restrict initial BH masses below the upper BH mass gap, $m_{\rm BH,ini} < 55\,{\rm M}_\odot$. Our analysis shows a strong BH spin-mass correlation, obtained within $\sim 10 \,{\rm Myr}$ when gas is depleted. Low-spin BHs, $a_{*} \leq 0.3$, are predominantly low-mass, $m_{\rm BH} \lesssim 25\,{\rm M}_\odot$, in contrast to high-spin black holes, $a_{*} \geq 0.7$, which are predominantly high-mass, $m_{\rm BH} \gtrsim 65\,{\rm M}_\odot$. Notably, there exist also low-spin, high-mass outliers with $\sim 1$ mass-gap BH per cluster expected to have $a_{*} \sim 0.1$. The general trend, however, expressed by the median spin as a function of final BH mass is well fit by a high-spin saturating exponential with transition mass $\sim 50\,{\rm M}_{\odot}$. For $m_{\rm BH} \geq 100\,{\rm M}_\odot$ the median spin is $\bar{a}_{*} \sim 0.90$ with the central $68\%$ of the distribution spanning $a_{*} \sim 0.70 - 0.96$, in striking agreement with the estimated spins of the gravitational-wave signal GW231123. These spin values persist up to the highest masses generated by our mechanism, $m_{\rm BH} \sim 10^3\,{\rm M}_\odot$.

AI.tldr: Proto-stellar clusters at high redshift enable stellar black holes to accrete gas, leading to a spin-mass correlation where high-mass BHs have high spins, matching gravitational-wave observations.
AI.motivation: To understand how compact proto-stellar clusters at high redshift (z~10) provide conditions for stellar black holes to accrete primordial gas and develop spin-mass correlations that can be tested against gravitational-wave observations.
AI.method: Develop a model of BH growth via gas accretion within compact gaseous proto-stellar clusters, accounting for velocity shear-induced accretion disks that are repeatedly disrupted by stochastic perturbations to BH motion. Assume low initial spins (a*,ini=0.01) and initial BH masses below the upper mass gap (<55 M☉).
AI.result: Strong BH spin-mass correlation emerges within ~10 Myr when gas is depleted: low-spin BHs (a*≤0.3) are predominantly low-mass (≲25 M☉), while high-spin BHs (a*≥0.7) are predominantly high-mass (≳65 M☉). Median spin follows high-spin saturating exponential with transition mass ~50 M☉. For m_BH≥100 M☉, median spin ~0.90 with 68% distribution spanning 0.70-0.96, matching GW231123 observations.
AI.conclusion: Compact proto-stellar clusters enable stellar BHs to grow via gas accretion, producing a distinctive spin-mass correlation where high-mass BHs achieve high spins. This mechanism explains observed gravitational-wave signals and predicts low-spin, high-mass outliers including mass-gap BHs.

title: Luminosity functions and IMF variations from large samples of HII regions and molecular clouds
comment: 12 pages, 11 figures
summary: Large high-quality samples of HII regions and their parent Giant Molecular Clouds (GMC) are now available for local galaxies. It is therefore possible to investigate links between the CO and H$α$ luminosity functions and whether massive stars form in GMCs of all masses. The CO luminosity functions (LF), representing the distribution of GMC masses, are consistently steeper than the H$α$ luminosity functions. The CO LF invariably steepens in the outer disk where fewer massive GMCs are present beyond the median cloud galactocentric distance. The H$α$ LF also steepens in the outer disk for most of the galaxies examined. Using Salpeter, Kroupa, and Chabrier Initial Mass Functions (IMF) along with stellar mass-luminosity-radius relations, we compute numerically the bolometric luminosity and H$α$ emission from young star clusters. The cluster masses are linked to the GMC mass by assuming that the cluster mass is a constant fraction (3\%) of the parent cloud mass. In particular, results for a fully stochastic IMF are compared to suggestions that very massive stars only form in massive clusters or clouds. Within the limits of the observations -- no small molecular clouds or low-luminosity HII regions can be detected at the typical $\sim 10$~Mpc distance of the sample galaxies -- we find no evidence for a maximum stellar mass which varies with cloud or cluster mass.

AI.tldr: The study analyzes CO and Hα luminosity functions in local galaxies to investigate whether massive star formation depends on GMC mass, finding no evidence for varying maximum stellar mass with cloud/cluster mass within observational limits.
AI.motivation: To investigate the relationship between GMC mass distribution (via CO luminosity functions) and massive star formation (via Hα luminosity functions), specifically testing whether massive stars form only in massive GMCs or clusters.
AI.method: Analyzed CO and Hα luminosity functions from local galaxies (~10 Mpc distance), compared their slopes, examined radial variations. Computed bolometric luminosity and Hα emission from young star clusters using Salpeter, Kroupa, and Chabrier IMFs with stellar mass-luminosity-radius relations, assuming cluster mass is 3% of parent GMC mass.
AI.result: CO luminosity functions are consistently steeper than Hα luminosity functions. Both functions steepen in outer galactic disks where fewer massive GMCs exist. Within observational limits (no detection of small molecular clouds or low-luminosity HII regions), no evidence found for maximum stellar mass varying with cloud or cluster mass.
AI.conclusion: Massive stars can form in GMCs of all masses, not just massive ones, contradicting suggestions that very massive stars only form in massive clusters or clouds. The findings support a fully stochastic IMF interpretation within current observational constraints.

title: Interaction-induced HI gas concentration with centrally-enhanced star formation in ALFALFA-SDSS galaxies
comment: 10 pages, 9 figures, submitted to MNRAS
summary: We present a statistical analysis for the interaction-induced central concentration of HI gas distributions and its connection with interaction-induced central star formation enhancement, using a large sample of $\sim 10^4$ galaxies from the ALFALFA and SDSS surveys. By adopting the HI profile parameter $K$, an indicator of gas concentration inferred from the integrated 21 cm emission line, we find that galaxies with more centrally concentrated HI (higher $K$ values) or enhanced specific star foramtion rate (sSFR) exhibit significantly stronger clustering and higher probability of hosting a nearby neighbor on scales below $100h^{-1}\mathrm{kpc}$, which is more pronounced in low-mass galaxies. Furthermore, by utilizing the enhancement functions for a sample of galaxy pairs, we directly trace the evolution of HI concentration and sSFR enhancement as a function of projected separation. Our findings indicate that tidal interactions drive a statistical synchrony between the central concentration of atomic gas and the enhancement of central star formation. Gas concentration appears to be a necessary condition for central star formation enhancement in interacting systems at all but the smallest separations. Compared to satellite galaxies, central galaxies exhibit stronger enhancement of gas fraction, gas concentration and sSFR, suggesting the role of environmental regulation.

AI.tldr: Statistical analysis shows tidal interactions drive synchronized central HI gas concentration and star formation enhancement in galaxies, with stronger effects in low-mass and central galaxies.
AI.motivation: To investigate the connection between interaction-induced central concentration of HI gas and central star formation enhancement in galaxies, and understand how tidal interactions synchronize these processes.
AI.method: Used large sample of ~10^4 galaxies from ALFALFA and SDSS surveys; adopted HI profile parameter K as gas concentration indicator from 21 cm emission; analyzed clustering and neighbor probability; used enhancement functions for galaxy pairs to trace evolution with projected separation.
AI.result: Galaxies with more centrally concentrated HI or enhanced sSFR show stronger clustering and higher probability of nearby neighbors (<100h⁻¹kpc), especially in low-mass galaxies. Tidal interactions drive statistical synchrony between HI concentration and star formation enhancement. Gas concentration appears necessary for central star formation enhancement at most separations. Central galaxies show stronger enhancement than satellites.
AI.conclusion: Tidal interactions statistically synchronize central HI gas concentration and star formation enhancement. Gas concentration is a necessary condition for interaction-induced central star formation. Environmental regulation plays a role, with central galaxies showing stronger enhancement effects than satellites.

title: Identifying AGNs from X-ray detections---I: Metallicity calibrations in AGNs with X-ray luminosity as the primary input parameter
comment: 20 pages, 8 figures, accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: We present the first semi-empirical strong-line calibrations to determine metallicity in Active Galactic Nuclei (AGNs) that use the directly observable X-ray luminosity ($Ł_{\rm X}$) instead of the dimensionless ionization parameter ($U$). The calibrations are derived from an extensive grid of photoionization models computed with the {\sc Cloudy} code, which are compared with observational data of Seyfert nuclei from the Burst Alert Telescope (BAT) AGN Spectroscopic Survey (BASS). In this first paper, we develop new calibrations for two key optical metallicity diagnostics based on the $N2$ and $O3N2$ indices, which are valid in a metallicity range of $8.0 \lesssim \logOH \lesssim 9.1\, {\rm or}\, 0.2 \lesssim (Z/Z_{\odot}) \lesssim 2.6$, with precision of $1σ\approx 0.22$ dex ($N2$) and $\approx 0.20$ dex ($O3N2$). We systematically investigate the influence of the AGN spectral index $(\aox)$, narrow-line region (NLR) gas density (\Ne), the characteristic peak temperature of the Big Blue Bump $(T_{\rm BB})$, and $Ł_{\rm X}$. We find a strong, opposing secondary dependence on $Ł_{\rm X}$ for both indices. We demonstrate that neglecting this parameter overlooks systematic offsets intrinsic to the diagnostics, leading to metallicity errors of up to $\sim 1.0$ dex, particularly for the least and most luminous sources. This framework offers a more precise characterization of chemical enrichment in the NLRs of AGNs by leveraging their intrinsic X-ray emission to mitigate these systematic biases.

AI.tldr: First semi-empirical strong-line calibrations for AGN metallicity using X-ray luminosity instead of ionization parameter, with N2 and O3N2 indices valid for 0.2-2.6 solar metallicity.
AI.motivation: Existing AGN metallicity calibrations rely on dimensionless ionization parameter (U), which is not directly observable. There's a need for calibrations using directly measurable X-ray luminosity (L_X) to reduce systematic biases and improve precision in characterizing chemical enrichment in AGN narrow-line regions.
AI.method: Developed extensive grid of photoionization models using Cloudy code, compared with observational data from BAT AGN Spectroscopic Survey (BASS). Created new calibrations for N2 and O3N2 indices, systematically investigating influence of AGN spectral index (α_OX), NLR gas density (N_e), Big Blue Bump temperature (T_BB), and L_X.
AI.result: Calibrations valid for 8.0 ≲ log(O/H) ≲ 9.1 (0.2-2.6 solar metallicity) with precision of 1σ ≈ 0.22 dex (N2) and ≈ 0.20 dex (O3N2). Found strong opposing secondary dependence on L_X for both indices. Neglecting L_X leads to metallicity errors up to ~1.0 dex, especially for least and most luminous sources.
AI.conclusion: Framework provides more precise characterization of chemical enrichment in AGN NLRs by leveraging intrinsic X-ray emission to mitigate systematic biases. Using directly observable L_X instead of U reduces uncertainties and enables more accurate metallicity determinations across wide luminosity ranges.

title: The structure and evolution of the Galactic high-$α$ disc I. Chemical and age orbital cartography
comment: 18 pages, 12 Figures, 1 Table. Submitted to MNRAS
summary: We present a comprehensive chemical and age orbital cartography of the Galactic high-$α$ disc using subgiant stars with precise ages, element abundances, and full phase-space information from the \textsl{LAMOST--Gaia} data set. Specifically, we map how average [Fe/H], [$α$/Fe], and age vary across present-day kinematic and orbital coordinates. We analyse the data in full and across mono-abundance populations to measure element abundance-orbital and age-orbital gradients across orbital actions and angular-momenta. Our results show that the high-$α$ disc exhibits clear and coherent gradients in [Fe/H], [$α$/Fe], and age with orbits; these gradients are much stronger and sharper in orbital space than in present-day kinematics, showing that orbital diagnostics recover the intrinsic disc structure of old disc populations more effectively than instantaneous kinematic coordinates. We find that older high-$α$ populations display qualitatively similar element abundance--orbital and age--orbital trends to stars in the low-$α$ disc, although the high-$α$ gradients are generally shallower. The presence of these ordered correlations indicates that the old high-$α$ disc is structured, and preserved a strong fossil record of its early assembly despite the Milky Way's subsequent accretion history. This result implies that later mergers did not fully erase the chemical-orbital and age-orbital structure imprinted during the high-$α$ disc's earliest formation epoch. All together, these findings indicate that the Galactic high-$α$ disc formed mainly through inside-out and upside-down growth.

AI.tldr: The study maps chemical and age gradients across orbital space in the Milky Way's high-α disc, revealing preserved fossil records of early formation despite subsequent mergers.
AI.motivation: To understand the formation history and structural preservation of the Galactic high-α disc by analyzing how chemical abundances and ages vary with orbital parameters, testing whether early assembly signatures survived subsequent galactic mergers.
AI.method: Used LAMOST-Gaia data set of subgiant stars with precise ages, element abundances, and full phase-space information. Mapped average [Fe/H], [α/Fe], and age variations across present-day kinematic and orbital coordinates. Analyzed data in full and across mono-abundance populations to measure element abundance-orbital and age-orbital gradients across orbital actions and angular momenta.
AI.result: High-α disc exhibits clear, coherent gradients in [Fe/H], [α/Fe], and age with orbits; gradients are stronger/sharp in orbital space than present-day kinematics. Older high-α populations show similar abundance-orbital trends to low-α disc but with shallower gradients. Ordered correlations indicate structured old high-α disc preserved strong fossil record of early assembly despite Milky Way's accretion history.
AI.conclusion: Later mergers did not fully erase chemical-orbital and age-orbital structure imprinted during high-α disc's earliest formation epoch. Findings indicate Galactic high-α disc formed mainly through inside-out and upside-down growth.
